speaker: interviewer
text: Klara Samuels, May 3, 1996. So you were liberated by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we're on this train, as I told you-- 5,000 Jews. And on the morning of April 13th, we are told that at three o'clock in the afternoon, there will be a roll call. And we're going to cross the River Elbe. The train could not cross it, because there was no bridge. So crossing meant swimming-- not a likely possibility of survival for 5,000 semi-starved people, weakened. And so everybody starts thinking about a strategy. The people who guarded this train were rather elderly men at that point. And we were running through the countryside. So some people decided to run off and hide. And some people just didn't know what to do. But before it happened, at about two o'clock, the first American units came out of the bushes. And I remember that one of them was a Jewish boy from Brooklyn, who literally gave away everything he had on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't know if I ever knew it, you know. I remember his first name was Abraham, but his last name I do not remember. The Americans came upon this group of Jews in the middle of sort of nowhere and were very efficient about it. They went into the surrounding villages. They made the women cook up some soup and bring it to us that same day. And next day, they ordered the local people to just take in whatever families or people showed up on their doorstep. The Germans, of course, were immediately-- with the exception of one or two-- completely unaware of anything that had been going on. And I think that-- that this-- very much their attitude-- was their attitude in the early years. Everybody you talked to denied having any connection with anything that had gone on. And in my opinion, they had to be-- they had to make themselves very blind not to see--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the Germans, are you talking about civilians, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am talking about civilians, though not necessarily just civilians. It could be also the military. Because after all, the majority of the German military were not the SS, but were the Wehrmacht, the fighting soldiers. But I think that there were so many camps in Germany, so many people who worked at the camps, obviously, they all talked. Obviously, they talked to their families. Obviously, this-- you really had to make a concerted effort to say, well, it's not my business. I want to survive myself. Even though I'm a decent person, there's nothing I can do about it-- very much an attitude that most people take even today, in relation to other things that are obviously not as terrible as what was happening to the Jews, but trying not to see the-- the ills of society.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. What happened to you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, now, we were liberated, as I told you. We're in that village the next day. And I speak English, so I am able to communicate with the American soldiers and officers. They-- the Americans immediately sent some doctors. Because many people were in a terrible condition. And in fact, so pitifully, I remember one family that all died in that period because they were so starved, they were unable-- their stomachs were unable to-- to absorb food. So here, to survive, and to die within the next few days. My father, being a doctor, was working in one of these little makeshift hospitals. And I, because I spoke English, was asked to go on the rounds with the American doctors who could not communicate with the patients. So I had immediately a sort of a job, of sorts, and-- and knew the-- the officers. And my father said to me-- there were rumors right away that that particular location, where we were, which was near the city of [? Magdeburg-- ?] some small town I don't remember the name of-- that that part of was really negotiated to go over to the Russians. And it was eventually exchanged and became part of East Germany. I think the River Elbe was-- was the border, or something like that. I don't remember. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were making rounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father said to me, let's try, as soon as we can, to go west. We don't want to go to Poland. My father realized, very astutely, that Poland was likely to fall under the influence of the-- of the Soviet Union. And he said, let's try to go west and get out of here. So I think I-- I did this work for approximately maybe three weeks or so. And at around May 7, just a day or two before V-E Day-- that's the end of the war in Europe-- there was a train available for French people to be repatriated to France. And I asked Captain [? Holland, ?] I remember, who was in charge of whatever then, to give me a pass to go to France. And he gave it to me. And I, my father, and 12 other people, none of whom could speak French, got on this train, which happened to be a cattle train, and we went west. All the other people were French-- they were women, French women. But the majority, I think, were young men. They were French prisoners of war. No borders-- we just went west. We went through Belgium. We went to France. And in the evening of the second day, we arrived in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you remained for--

speaker: interviewee
text: We're at the station. This was evening, and there were three organizations that showed up at the station. There was an organization representing-- among others, you know-- organization representing the communist Poland, an organization representing the [NON-ENGLISH] Polish government, and an organization called the Joint Committee, which was a Jewish organization, a charitable Jewish organization. Naturally, we went with them. And they-- they housed us in some kind of a dormitory arrangement. And we were in Paris for about three or four weeks. It was right after the war-- still living life of refugees, but fortunately, not in a refugee camp. Not normal lives, you know, being on somebody's charity, in a big room-- 14 people sleeping together. Walking in the streets of Paris, looking into apartments, where people had dining rooms, where people sat down to a dinner with a family, where people went to hairdressers and wore reasonable clothes-- you know, when we arrived in the camp, I had one dress that I wore. And we bought one other dress-- one for the winter, one for the summer-- one pair of shoes, I think one or two changes of underwear. That is all I had, you know. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it's just such a different life. It's very difficult to adapt to normalcy. You feel almost that you don't belong in a normal way of life, you know. You are used to such terrible things. I, for example-- in the camp, there was a big sort of a-- I don't know if you call it a bathroom. I guess a washroom, you would call it-- a row of sinks, a row of latrines, all in view. A few people, of whom I was one, who would go in the morning, in the worst weather, and strip and wash, so I guess I must have had a towel or something to dry with. And all of a sudden, you know, there's a bathroom. It took me months to be able to use the bathroom alone. I just was not used to any privacy. Things that are so normal that you lose-- so walking the street of Paris, in a way, you think that when it's over, jubilation. But it's not. Because very few people were lucky enough to go immediately-- to be saved and go immediately to normal life. And even when you do go to normal life, it is hard to adapt. I, personally, was not consumed by guilt, which I know is the experience of many survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had other feelings.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but you have a feeling of inferiority because you-- you don't belong with normal people. It takes a long time to get used to it, to overcome it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you, or did you, feel that way? Were you told that you were not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, you feel that way because you don't have the things that other people have, because you are living in a-- in a dormitory, and you don't own a change of clothes, and you don't-- you're not going to school. You're not living the normal life of other people. Fortunately, we were in France a rather short period of time. Because in Palestine, at the time, there was a quota for how many Jews could immigrate every year. And the quota for 1945 was empty. There was no place from which Jews could immigrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So being one of the first, the Joint Committee arranged for us official papers to go to-- to Palestine. After about three weeks, we went by train to Marseilles and waited there a couple more weeks for a ship and went legally to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Palestine for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I didn't speak any Hebrew. And to go to a Hebrew school-- a very difficult language, Hebrew. It would take me probably at least four or five years to graduate high school, and I was close to 18 already. So instead, I had an uncle in New York, who invited me to come to college in United States. And so I studied privately-- again, in Israel. So still was not part of-- of normal life. You know, the first year I was living-- I had an aunt. And I was living in my aunt's apartment. My father was in Jerusalem. I was in Tel Aviv. He had a job in Jerusalem. And I was still sort of living on charity. They treated me very well, no question, like one of the members of the family. But nevertheless, I felt it. I was different. My cousin, who was nine-- who was 18 months younger than I, whom I had known in Poland, and who became good friend when I went to Palestine-- she was going to school. She had friends. She tried to include me. But it was not easy. I didn't belong. My experiences were different. I was not lighthearted at the time, at all. I remember-- although I did the things, some of the things that teenagers did, I listened to the music, and so on-- I know that my heart wasn't in it at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father doing at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father spent a year working for an organization called [NON-ENGLISH], which is I guess like Blue Cross Blue Shield in the United States, and learning about subtropical diseases and so on. And after a year, we used the $8,000 that had been in the United States waiting for us-- my uncle, having charge of it, who sent it to us-- sent it to us, to Palestine. And we got an apartment. My father organized a laboratory. And I was-- I had studied for a year, took the exams, and then takes months to get the results. Because these exams, called London Matriculation, kind of like equivalency exams in United States. You take them, then they go to England to the University of London, and it takes months before you get the results. And at the same time, I was already applying to universities in United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the second year, I was working with my father, as his assistant, and hoping to get into a university in United States, and I did. Just briefly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did the $8,000 get to the United States, to your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really don't know. I know how it got to [? Vilna, ?] through people who would carry the money. And I think my uncle brought it out when he came. This is the uncle who lived in New York City-- brought it out. And he kept it for us, you know, and made it available when we needed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry to digress.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK, that's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you ended up at-- at the university in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got accepted to two universities, NYU and Columbia. And I looked at the map of Manhattan. My uncle lived on 87th Street and West End Avenue, which was closer to Columbia, so I went Columbia, never knowing that it was the better of the two schools. So I came and lived in my uncle's apartment and went to school and graduated with a degree in chemical engineering. I worked in New York for several years. And eventually, in-- in '55, 1955, my husband and I-- we got married, and we moved to Buffalo, where he worked for the then Allied Chemical, and which later became Allied Signal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he a chemist as well, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a chemical engineer, and I graduated as a chemical engineer. We met at work. And we had two boys in Buffalo. And eventually, he was transferred to this area, and we moved to this house, where we are now close to 30 years, and where I became-- went back to school, got the certification for high school teaching, and became a physics and chemistry teacher and worked for 26 years at Livingston High School and retired two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I understand you've been keeping busy since retirement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. We actually-- I retired to keep busy with other things. Because you know, when I started working, I had a lot of energy for other things. And I had other-- as a matter of fact, I even had a little business. I was doing technical translations on the side, while I was teaching and running the house, and transporting the kids to Sunday school and Hebrew school and various lessons and so on. We did some traveling. And my hobbies at the time were playing some bridge. And-- but I didn't have too much time, as you can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: You taught bridge, didn't you? Or am I mistaken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, I did not. I-- but towards the end of my work, we became interested, my husband and I-- the last 12 years or so, we became interested in ballroom dancing. And eventually, we danced competitively and-- we have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see a lot of trophies on your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there's a whole-- a whole series of trophies. --shelf over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: A shelf of trophies, yes. Well, you know, in that business, they try to give trophies to as many people as they can. [LAUGHS] But we're pretty good. And-- but the last few years of work, I really-- I got older, and I was too tired to do other things. And there were many things I wanted to do, so I retired almost two years ago. And since then, I've been doing a lot of things. It's another big break in my life. I-- I do volunteer work. And they always have me doing something that has to do with teaching. I teach English to Russian emigre. I gave a series of lectures on physics to a group of senior citizens, average age about 80. I do book reviews for a residence kind of home. I take courses myself. I love to go to school. And you belong to a French club?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I belong to two French organizations, a French club, and Alliance Francaise de Montclair. I play duplicate bridge. I go to the gym three times a week. We travel. We go to New York a lot, my husband and I. We go-- we have series at the opera and the ballet, a theater series. So we like, all-- sort of go a lot to museums. My husband, who was a chemical engineer, has since become an artist. He retired about eight to nine years before me. Of course, he's standing over there.

words.text: Of course, he's standing over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand you have a trip planned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we are going to Poland. It's my first time since the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think that it will be easy, but I think it's a kind of closure that I need and was not able to bring myself to do before. I found it very difficult to talk about my experiences. I married an American man, which is, as a matter of fact, rather rare among survivors. But it was the best thing for me to have done. Because until then, I was very much connected to my experiences. When I was in Israel, people always-- everybody had somebody whom they lost. Everybody wanted to talk about it. It kept me on the subject, even when I was in school at Columbia University. I had many friends. I had many friends who were American by then, but also friends who were Jews, Polish Jews, survivors, refugees. I belonged to a-- an interfaith club at Columbia. I was still-- I belong to [NON-ENGLISH], which was a Zionist club. I was still very connected to the war. But when we married and moved to Buffalo, by necessity, I had a new family. I had a different job. I-- I-- that's when I started teaching. I taught for two years in a college in Buffalo. And after that, I had my family retired and brought them. I brought up my family-- removed me from it. And I suppose I-- perhaps I took the easy way out. I tried not to think about it. Gradually, it became somewhat dreamlike. And probably, it helped me to survive. And probably, it help-- it helped also my children who, although they considered me always very domineering and bossy-- [LAUGHS] which I am-- did not have quite the syndrome that we have since read about the children of survivors. You know, they did not feel the guilt because I did not live the guilt. You know, maybe it's there somewhere in my personality, but I don't feel it.

words.text: personality, but I don't feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think the Holocaust changed you from the way you might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to tell because I was a child when it started. So you-- who knows what I would have been like if it wasn't for the Holocaust? Maybe a better person, maybe not-- probably a more shallow person. You know, it-- it-- surviving doesn't qualify you for understanding the Holocaust. Even though you have experienced it, it doesn't mean that you have necessarily a deeper understanding of it. But perhaps it makes you think about it more later, when-- when you are an adult and you sort of can't help but review these events.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have an analytical mind. I always try to understand things. And I try to think and understand how such an event could have happened. I don't have the answers. Nobody has, of course. And you know, if you think about it, either way, either you think that there are some peoples who are capable of such deeds, who are predisposed to such deeds, or if you think that everybody is capable of it given a certain history and a certain set of circumstances, I don't know what's worse. Which way it is-- to think about it is worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I do think that even if there are some people predisposed, or peoples, it comes out if they feel aggrieved and humiliated. Happy people don't go around murdering other people. As time goes on, I am tending to think in more universal terms about it because small holocausts have happened since, and they have happened in various parts of the world. You almost have to think that there is something in the human nature. It's-- no matter how you think about it, you cannot help but be pessimistic about the fact that this can always crop up given certain history and circumstances. And because of that, I think, it becomes so important to learn about it, to know about it, to try to teach one's children so they will never become [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Holocaust initiators in some part of the world, in some time. And that's the best I can say about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess it's education.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is. It is-- it is more than education. It is a way we, ourselves, live. Because we do more to educate our children in matters of morals by example than by conscious direction that we try to give them. They-- they react to us in either rebelling or following or even a combination of both. I think it's a lot more subconscious. So it is-- it is therefore a slow process, but we do have to try the best we can. And one of the ways in which we try is to tell the story of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is what you have done today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is what I'm-- I'm doing and which is why I am doing it. Because as I say, I don't like to talk about it. But I feel an obligation. I've talked about it to groups, and particularly to groups of young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which I think is very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klara, I think we've about concluded. Unless you have something to add, I'd like to show your pictures and introduce your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, very good.
